instruction:
The Question Paper contains three sections.
 Section A has 25 questions. Attempt any 20 questions.
 Section B has 24 questions. Attempt any 20 questions.
 Section C has 6 questions. Attempt any 5 questions.
 All questions carry equal marks.
 There is no negative marking.

questions.question: Maximum number of electrons occupied by s,p, and d orbitals are:
questions.question_html: Maximum number of electrons occupied by s,p, and d orbitals are:
questions.explanation:
s-orbital ml=0, so it can accommodate 2 electron.

p-orbital has ml = -1,0,+1. It can accommodate 6 electrons.

d-orbital has ml = -2,-1,0,1,2.It can accommodate 10 electrons.

questions.question: Select the species which are iso-electronic (same number of the electron) with each other.
questions.question_html: Select the species which are iso-electronic (same number of the electron) with each other.
questions.explanation:
Neutral Cl ( Z=17) has 17 electrons and with the addition of one more electron, it becomes Cl^(-) anion which has 18 electrons.

Neutral Ca (Z=20) has 20 electrons and with the removal of 2 electrons, it becomes Ca^(2+) cation which has 18 electrons.

Since Cl^(- ) and Ca^(2+) both has 18 electrons, so they are isoelectronic.

questions.question:
Water behaves as an amphoteric substance because:

 it has the ability to act as an acid.
 it has the ability to act as a base.
 it has the ability to act as an acid as well as a base.
 it has the ability to oxidize as well as reduce.
questions.question_html:
Water behaves as an amphoteric substance because:

  1. it has the ability to act as an acid.
  2. it has the ability to act as a base.
  3. it has the ability to act as an acid as well as a base.
  4. it has the ability to oxidize as well as reduce.
questions.explanation:
An amphoteric compound is a compound that can act either as an acid or as a base in a chemical reaction. The first example of water's amphoteric character is the fact that it can self-ionize to a small extent. A water molecule is capable of donating one of its protons (H^(+)) to a neighbouring water molecule, forming the hydroxide and hydronium ions.

 $H_2O+H_2O\rightleftharpoons H_3O^++OH^-$

questions.question:
Acid catalysed electrophilic addition reaction to carbon-carbon double bond proceeds in two steps. The first step involves the addition of an H^(+ )to double bond. Name the type of intermediate formed in the first step of the following addition reaction:

 ${{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{C}}-{\text{HC }} = {\text{ C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}} + {\text{ }}{{\text{H}}^ + } \to ?$
questions.question_html:
Acid catalysed electrophilic addition reaction to carbon-carbon double bond proceeds in two steps. The first step involves the addition of an H+ to double bond. Name the type of intermediate formed in the first step of the following addition reaction:
{tex}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{C}}-{\text{HC }} = {\text{ C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}} + {\text{ }}{{\text{H}}^ + } \to ?{/tex}
questions.explanation: In this reaction, formation of secondary carbocation takes place by the addition of H^(+) to terminal carbon of carbon-carbon double bond among the two possible carbon atoms. The more stable secondary carbocation formation will take place instead of primary carbocation.

questions.question: Charge on the electron was determined by
questions.question_html: Charge on the electron was determined by

options.text: Electrical discharge carried out in the modified cathode ray tube
options.html: Electrical discharge carried out in the modified cathode ray tube

questions.explanation:
In 1909, Robert Millikan and Harvey Fletcher conducted the oil drop experiment to determine the charge of an electron. They suspended tiny charged droplets of oil between two metal electrodes by balancing downward gravitational force with upward drag and electric forces.

The experiment helped earn Millikan a Nobel prize in 1923

questions.question: According to Gay Lussac’s Law of Gaseous Volumes, " when gases combine or are produced in a chemical reaction they do so in a ______ ratio by volumes provided all gases are at the same temperature and pressure."
questions.question_html: According to Gay Lussac’s Law of Gaseous Volumes, " when gases combine or are produced in a chemical reaction they do so in a ______ ratio  by volumes provided all gases are at the same temperature and pressure."
questions.explanation:
Gay lussac’s law states that when gases combine or are produced in a chemical reaction they do so in a simple ratio by volume provided all gases are at the same temperature and pressure.

Mathematically, the law is expressed as,

[V/n] = k

where V represents the volume of the gas, n denotes the number of moles of the gas & k is the proportionality constant.

Thus, the volume ratio must be related to the relative number of molecules.

This relationship was crucial in establishing the formula of simple molecules at a time when a distinction between atoms and molecules was not clearly understood.

questions.question: Television pictures result due to
questions.question_html: Television pictures result due to

options.text: phosphorescence of protons on the television screen
options.html: phosphorescence of protons on the television screen

options.text: fluorescence of cathode rays on the television screen
options.html: fluorescence of cathode rays on the television screen

options.text: fluorescence of protons on the television screen
options.html: fluorescence of protons on the television screen

options.text: fluorescence of canal rays on the television screen
options.html: fluorescence of canal rays on the television screen

correct_options: fluorescence of cathode rays on the television screen
questions.explanation:
Cathode rays (also called an electron beam or e-beam) are streams of electrons observed in vacuum tubes. If an evacuated glass tube is equipped with two electrodes and a voltage is applied, the glass behind of the positive electrode is observed to glow, due to electrons emitted from and traveling away from the cathode (the electrode connected to the negative terminal of the voltage supply).

Cathode ray tubes (CRTs) use a focused beam of electrons deflected by electric or magnetic fields to create the image in a television set.

questions.question: Displacement of hydrogen from cold water is done by:
questions.question_html: Displacement of hydrogen from cold water is done by:
questions.explanation:
A common characteristic of most Alkali metal is their ability to displace H_(2) (g) from water. This is represented by their large, negative electrode potentials. In this event, the Group 1 metal is oxidized to its metal ion, and water is reduced to form hydrogen gas and hydroxide ions. The general reaction of an alkali metal (M) with H_(2)O (l) is given in the following equation:

2M(s) + 2H_(2)O(l) $\longrightarrow$ 2M + (aq) + 2OH−(aq) + H_(2)(g)

From this reaction it is apparent that OH- is produced, creating a basic or alkaline environment. Group 1 elements are called alkali metals because of their ability to displace H_(2) (g) from water and create a basic solution. Alkali metals are also known to react violently and explosively with water. This is because enough heat is given off during the exothermic reaction to ignite the H_(2)(g).

questions.question: One mole of CO_(2 )contains
questions.question_html: One mole of CO2 contains
questions.explanation:
Since , as per Avogadro's law one mole of CO_(2) contains 6.022 $\times$ 10^(23) molecules & the number of atoms of carbon per molecule of CO_(2) = 1

 $\therefore$ the number of atoms of carbon in 6.022 $\times$ 10^(23) molecules of CO_(2)

= 6.022 $\times$ 10^(23 )atoms of C

questions.question: The Kelvin scale is related to celsius scale as:
questions.question_html: The Kelvin scale is related to celsius scale as:
questions.explanation:
The relation between the Kelvin scale & Celcius scale of temperatures is -

K = °C + 273.15

Such a relationship is based upon the experimental findings and subsequent conclusion of Charle's law.

The Kelvin scale is also termed as an "absolute scale of temperature."

It is interesting to note that temperature below 0^(o)C ( ie. negative values ) is possible in the Celcius scale but in Kelvin scale, the negative temperature is not possible.

questions.question: Fractional Oxidation Number exists because:
questions.question_html: Fractional Oxidation Number exists because:

options.text: the element is present in different oxidation states.
options.html: the element is present in different oxidation states.

options.text: middle carbon in such elements has always zero oxidation state.
options.html: middle carbon in such elements has always zero oxidation state.

options.text: the average oxidation state of the element is divided into two.
options.html: the average oxidation state of the element is divided into two.

options.text: electrons are never shared/transferred in that element.
options.html: electrons are never shared/transferred in that element.

correct_options: the element is present in different oxidation states.
questions.explanation:
The oxidation states are usually in whole numbers, but in some cases, they are in fractional numbers. Consider Fe_(3)O_(4).

 Using rule #2, the sum of the oxidation state of all atoms is 0
 Using rule #6, the O has the oxidation state of -2. 4 $\times$ (-2) = -8
 Equation: 3Fe + (-8) = 0
 3Fe = 8
 Fe = $8 \over 3$ 

In most cases, atoms of the same element in a given compound have the same oxidation states, but each atom can have a different state than the other. For Fe_(3)O_(4), two Fe atoms have an oxidation state of +3 and one of +2, which makes the total oxidation state of Fe= $8 \over 3$ .

Instead of averaging the oxidation states, we must keep them separated. This is called fragmenting, which occurs if there is an ionic compound, and the ions can be separated. The oxidation state of the individual atoms in the different ions can be determined.

H3C—HC = CH2 + H+{tex}\rightarrow{/tex} ?

When the electrophile attacks CH_(3)-CH=CH_(2), delocalisation of electron can take place in two ways:

questions.explanation: Ionic crystalline compounds formed by ion-formation by electron transfer proves the electronic theory of chemical bonding. According to this theory, ionic bonds are formed when an atom loses electron/ electrons to become a positive ion and another atom gains the electron/ electrons to become a negative ion. questions.question_html: Radioactive elements emit {tex}\alpha{/tex}, {tex}\beta{/tex} and {tex}\gamma{/tex} rays and are characterised by their halflives. The radioactive isotope of hydrogen is:
questions.explanation: The tritium concentration is about one atom per 10^(18) atoms of protium. of these isotopes, the only tritium is radioactive and emits low energy beta particles(t_(1/2), 12.33years).

questions.question: In sulphur estimation, 0.157 g of an organic compound gave 0.4813 g of barium sulphate. What is the percentage of sulphur in the compound?
questions.question_html: In sulphur estimation, 0.157 g of an organic compound gave 0.4813 g of barium sulphate. What is the percentage of sulphur in the compound?
questions.explanation:
Molecular mass of BaSO_(4 )= 137 + 32 + 64 g = 233 g

233 g BaSO_(4) contains 32 g S

0.4813 g BaSO_(4 )contains $\frac{{(32 \times 0.4813g)}}{{233\;S}}$ 

So % of S = $ \frac{{(32 \times 0.4813 \times 100)}}{{(233 \times 0.157)}}$ 

S = 42.10%

questions.question: Amount of sodium hydroxide present in 500ml of 0.2M solutions is :
questions.question_html: Amount of sodium hydroxide present in 500ml of 0.2M solutions is :
questions.explanation:
Molarity of a solution

(M) = $\frac{mass of solute}{molar mass of solute } \times $ $\frac{1000}{volume of solution in mL } $ 

∴0.2​ $=\frac{w}{40 } \times $ $\frac{1000}{500} $ 

so, w = 4gm.

Amount of sodium hydroxide present in 500ml of 0.2M solutions is 4 gm.

questions.question:
H_(2)O_(2) behaves as a bleaching agent because:

 it breaks the chemical bonds of the chromophores.
 acts as a strong reducing agent in acidic media.
 acts as a strong reducing agent in basic media.
 it absorbs the visible light.
questions.question_html:
H2O2 behaves as a bleaching agent because:

  1. it breaks the chemical bonds of the chromophores.
  2. acts as a strong reducing agent in acidic media.
  3. acts as a strong reducing agent in basic media.
  4. it absorbs the visible light.
questions.explanation: Because it can undergo bond dissociation randomly in the presence of light. It dissociates and generates free radicals which are very reactive and act as a bleaching agent.

questions.question: A solution is prepared by adding 2 g of a substance A to 18 g of water. Mass percent of the solute is _______.
questions.question_html: A solution is prepared by adding 2 g of a substance A to 18 g of water. Mass percent of the solute is _______.
questions.explanation:
Since , mass % =[{(mass of solute) $\times$ ) / (mass of solution ) } $\times$ 100]

 $\therefore$ Substituting the given values we get,

Mass per cent

= [ ( 2 g ) / { (2 g of A ) + 18 g of water } ]

= [ ( 2 /20 ) x 100 ]

= 10%

questions.question: During the formation of a chemical bond:
questions.question_html: During the formation of a chemical bond:

options.text: energy of the system does not change
options.html: energy of the system does not change

options.text: electron-electron repulsion becomes more than the nucleus-electron attraction
options.html: electron-electron repulsion becomes more than the nucleus-electron attraction

questions.explanation: A chemical bond is a lasting attraction between atoms that enables the formation of chemical compounds. The bond may result from the electrostatic force of attraction between atoms with opposite charges, or through the sharing of electrons as in the covalent bonds. When a bond forms, electrons are attracted to the space between nuclei where the electrostatic force of attraction is greater. As the electrons fall to a position of lower potential energy, the total mechanical energy of the molecular system decreases. Part of the mechanical energy of the unbound atoms is lost when they form the molecular system with a lower total mechanical energy.

questions.question:
What does the following diagram showing Lewis structures depict?

 ![image](https://media-mycbseguide.s3.amazonaws.com/images/testpaper/1.04.11.12.69.Ques.jpg)
questions.question_html: What does the following diagram showing Lewis structures depict?
questions.explanation: These are resonating structures of nitromethane. It is known that the two N-O bonds of nitromethane are of the same length (intermediate between a N-O single bond and a N=O double bond). The actual structure of nitromethane is, therefore, a resonance hybrid of the two canonical forms I and II.

questions.explanation: The emission spectrum of hydrogen consists of several series of sharp emission lines in the ultraviolet (Lyman series). in the visible (Balmer series), and in the infrared (Paschen series, Brackett series, etc,) regions of the spectrum. questions.question: The energy associated with the first orbit in the hydrogen atom is -2.17 $\times$ 10^(-18) J/atom. What is the energy associated with the fifth orbit?
questions.question_html: The energy associated with the first orbit in the hydrogen atom is -2.17 {tex}\times{/tex}10-18 J/atom. What is the energy associated with the fifth orbit?
questions.explanation:
The energy of first (Bohr) orbit in hydrogen atom = -2.17 $\times$ 10^(-18)Jatom^(-1)

The energy of the fifth orbit will be given by $E_n= E_1\times {Z^2\over N^2}$ 

 $E_5={-2.17\times 10^{-18}\over 5^2}=$ 8.68 $\times$ 10^(-20)Jatom^(-1)

Reason (R): In this molecule all the carbon atoms are attached to each other by double bonds.
questions.question_html:
Assertion (A): All the carbon atoms in H2C = C = CH2 are sp2 hybridised.
Reason (R): In this molecule all the carbon atoms are attached to each other by double bonds.

questions.explanation:
Hybridization of C can be find out by counting $\sigma$ bonds and $\pi$ bonds present on C atom.

 $\underset{1\pi }{\overset{3\sigma }{\mathop{C{{H}_{2}}}}}\,=\underset{2\pi }{\overset{2\sigma }{\mathop{C}}}\,=\underset{1\pi }{\overset{3\sigma }{\mathop{C{{H}_{2}}}}}\,$ 

If C has 3 $\sigma$ bonds, it is sp^(2) hybridized. If C has 2 $\sigma$ bonds, it is sp hybridized.

questions.explanation:
Molecular formula is the exact no. of atoms present in a molecule of a compound.

Molecular formula of a compound is related with its empirical formula as,

Mollecular formula = (Empirical formula)_(n)

where n represents a positive integer.

questions.question: de-Broglie equation is
questions.question_html: de-Broglie equation is
questions.explanation:
Louis de-Broglie proposed that matter, like light , has a dual character.It exhibits wave as well as particle nature. The wavelength of the wave associated with a particle of mass m moving with velocity v is given by

 $\lambda$ = $\frac{h}{m v}$

questions.question:
In the four compounds below, which of the following pairs are position isomers?

 $C{H_3} - C{H_2} - C{H_2} - C{H_2} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - H$ 
 $C{H_3} - C{H_2} - C{H_2} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - C{H_3}$ 
 $C{H_3} - C{H_2} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_{||} }\limits_O - C{H_2} - C{H_3}$ 
 $C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_| H}\limits_{C{H_3}} - C{H_2} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_{||} }\limits_O - H$
questions.question_html:
In the four compounds below, which of the following pairs are position isomers?

  1. {tex}C{H_3} - C{H_2} - C{H_2} - C{H_2} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - H{/tex}
  2. {tex}C{H_3} - C{H_2} - C{H_2} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - C{H_3}{/tex}
  3. {tex}C{H_3} - C{H_2} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_{||} }\limits_O - C{H_2} - C{H_3}{/tex}
  4. {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_| H}\limits_{C{H_3}} - C{H_2} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_{||} }\limits_O - H{/tex}
questions.explanation: B and C are position isomers as they have the same number of C atoms and same functional group -C=O but the position of carbonyl group is C-2 in B and C-3 in C.

questions.question:
Instructions for Question 53 to 55:
The phenomenon of the existence of two or more compounds possessing the same molecular formula but different properties is known as isomerism. Such compounds are called isomers. Compounds having the same molecular formula but different structures (manners in which atoms are linked) are classified as structural isomers. Structural isomers are classified as chain isomer, position isomer, functional group isomer. Meristematic arises due to different alkyl chains on either side of the functional group in the molecule and stereoisomerism and can be classified as geometrical and optical isomerism. Hyperconjugation is a general stabilising interaction. It involves delocalisation of σ electrons of the C-H bond of an alkyl group directly attached to an atom of an unsaturated system or to an atom with an unshared p orbital. This type of overlap stabilises the carbocation because electron density from the adjacent σ bond helps in dispersing the positive charge.

Assertion (A): Isopentane, pentane and Neopentane are chain isomers.

Reason (R): They have a similar molecular formula but a different carbon skeleton.
questions.question_html:
Instructions for Question 53 to 55:

The phenomenon of the existence of two or more compounds possessing the same molecular formula but different properties is known as isomerism. Such compounds are called isomers. Compounds having the same molecular formula but different structures (manners in which atoms are linked) are classified as structural isomers. Structural isomers are classified as chain isomer, position isomer, functional group isomer. Meristematic arises due to different alkyl chains on either side of the functional group in the molecule and stereoisomerism and can be classified as geometrical and optical isomerism. Hyperconjugation is a general stabilising interaction. It involves delocalisation of σ electrons of the C-H bond of an alkyl group directly attached to an atom of an unsaturated system or to an atom with an unshared p orbital. This type of overlap stabilises the carbocation because electron density from the adjacent σ bond helps in dispersing the positive charge.


Assertion (A): Isopentane, pentane and Neopentane are chain isomers.
Reason (R): They have a similar molecular formula but a different carbon skeleton.

questions.question:
Instructions for Question 53 to 55:
The phenomenon of the existence of two or more compounds possessing the same molecular formula but different properties is known as isomerism. Such compounds are called isomers. Compounds having the same molecular formula but different structures (manners in which atoms are linked) are classified as structural isomers. Structural isomers are classified as chain isomer, position isomer, functional group isomer. Meristematic arises due to different alkyl chains on either side of the functional group in the molecule and stereoisomerism and can be classified as geometrical and optical isomerism. Hyperconjugation is a general stabilising interaction. It involves delocalisation of σ electrons of the C-H bond of an alkyl group directly attached to an atom of an unsaturated system or to an atom with an unshared p orbital. This type of overlap stabilises the carbocation because electron density from the adjacent σ bond helps in dispersing the positive charge.

Assertion (A): $CHCH_3^{2+}$ (ethyl cation) has a positively charged carbon atom has an empty porbital.

Reason (R): Hyperconjugation is not possible in alkenes and alkylarenes.
questions.question_html:
Instructions for Question 53 to 55:

The phenomenon of the existence of two or more compounds possessing the same molecular formula but different properties is known as isomerism. Such compounds are called isomers. Compounds having the same molecular formula but different structures (manners in which atoms are linked) are classified as structural isomers. Structural isomers are classified as chain isomer, position isomer, functional group isomer. Meristematic arises due to different alkyl chains on either side of the functional group in the molecule and stereoisomerism and can be classified as geometrical and optical isomerism. Hyperconjugation is a general stabilising interaction. It involves delocalisation of σ electrons of the C-H bond of an alkyl group directly attached to an atom of an unsaturated system or to an atom with an unshared p orbital. This type of overlap stabilises the carbocation because electron density from the adjacent σ bond helps in dispersing the positive charge.


Assertion (A): {tex}CHCH_3^{2+}{/tex} (ethyl cation) has a positively charged carbon atom has an empty porbital.
Reason (R): Hyperconjugation is not possible in alkenes and alkylarenes.

questions.question:
Instructions for Question 53 to 55:
The phenomenon of the existence of two or more compounds possessing the same molecular formula but different properties is known as isomerism. Such compounds are called isomers. Compounds having the same molecular formula but different structures (manners in which atoms are linked) are classified as structural isomers. Structural isomers are classified as chain isomer, position isomer, functional group isomer. Meristematic arises due to different alkyl chains on either side of the functional group in the molecule and stereoisomerism and can be classified as geometrical and optical isomerism. Hyperconjugation is a general stabilising interaction. It involves delocalisation of σ electrons of the C-H bond of an alkyl group directly attached to an atom of an unsaturated system or to an atom with an unshared p orbital. This type of overlap stabilises the carbocation because electron density from the adjacent σ bond helps in dispersing the positive charge.

Assertion (A): C_(4)H_(10)O represents methoxypropane (CH_(3)OC_(3)H_(7)) and ethoxyethane (C_(2)H_(5)OC_(2)H_(5)). 

Reason (R): Stereoisomerism can be classified as geometrical and optical isomerism.
questions.question_html:
Instructions for Question 53 to 55:

The phenomenon of the existence of two or more compounds possessing the same molecular formula but different properties is known as isomerism. Such compounds are called isomers. Compounds having the same molecular formula but different structures (manners in which atoms are linked) are classified as structural isomers. Structural isomers are classified as chain isomer, position isomer, functional group isomer. Meristematic arises due to different alkyl chains on either side of the functional group in the molecule and stereoisomerism and can be classified as geometrical and optical isomerism. Hyperconjugation is a general stabilising interaction. It involves delocalisation of σ electrons of the C-H bond of an alkyl group directly attached to an atom of an unsaturated system or to an atom with an unshared p orbital. This type of overlap stabilises the carbocation because electron density from the adjacent σ bond helps in dispersing the positive charge.


Assertion (A): C4H10O represents methoxypropane (CH3OC3H7) and ethoxyethane (C2H5OC2H5). 
Reason (R): Stereoisomerism can be classified as geometrical and optical isomerism.
